text: Long before Rome or Athens rose to power, the ancient kingdom of Colchis thrived on the eastern shores of the Black Sea. Greek writers called it a land of untold wealth and dark mystery.

The Colchian people mastered gold mining centuries before most civilizations. They used sheep fleeces to trap gold dust from mountain streams — a technique that may have inspired the legend of the Golden Fleece.

The myth of Jason and the Argonauts describes a Greek hero sailing to Colchis to seize the Golden Fleece. Whether myth or distorted memory of real trade voyages, the story reveals how legendary Colchis was to the ancient world.

King Aeëtes, ruler of Colchis in Greek mythology, was said to guard the Golden Fleece in a sacred grove. He was also the father of Medea, one of the most powerful and feared women in all of Greek legend.
Medea, princess of Colchis, was renowned for her knowledge of poisons, herbs, and magic. In the myths she helped Jason obtain the Golden Fleece, then took a terrible revenge when he betrayed her.

The Rioni River was the great artery of ancient Colchis, carrying gold from the Caucasus Mountains down to the Black Sea coast. Its fertile valley supported one of the most productive agricultural regions in the ancient world.
Colchian merchants traded gold, bronze tools, timber, and linen across the Black Sea. Greek colonies along the coast, such as Dioscurias (modern Sukhumi), became bustling hubs of East-West exchange.

The city of Vani, perched on a hilltop overlooking the Colchian plain, was a major political and religious center. Excavations have revealed rich burials with gold ornaments, Greek pottery, and ritual bronze figurines.

Colchian bronze workers were among the finest in the ancient world, crafting axes, daggers, and elaborate ornaments. Their distinctive geometric patterns influenced art across the wider region for centuries.
Colchian warriors were feared in battle, fighting with distinctive bronze axes and tall wicker shields. Greek writers noted their dark hair and complexions, differentiating them from northern tribes.

The Black Sea was known to the Greeks as 'Pontus Euxinus' — the Hospitable Sea. For Colchian sailors, however, it was simply home, and their sturdy vessels navigated its waters long before Greek colonists arrived.

Agriculture flourished in Colchis thanks to abundant rainfall and rich soil. The Greeks were astonished by the region's crops of millet, wheat, and grapes, which grew with almost no irrigation needed.

Colchian religious practices blended ancestor worship with nature cults. Sacred groves, river spirits, and serpent symbolism played central roles in a spiritual tradition that predated Greek influence by millennia.

The legend of the Golden Fleece may preserve a real memory of Colchian gold-mining techniques. Mountain streams still carry gold flakes today, and locals in Svaneti have used fleece-trapping methods into the modern era.
The Argonautica, written by Apollonius of Rhodes in the 3rd century BC, is the fullest surviving account of the voyage to Colchis. It paints the kingdom as wealthy, dangerous, and utterly enchanting.

Colchis maintained its independence through a combination of natural defenses — mountains, rivers, and dense forests — and military strength. No foreign power would fully conquer the region until the Roman era.

The Colchian script, preserved on a handful of inscriptions, remains undeciphered to this day. It suggests a literate society with its own distinct intellectual tradition, separate from both Greek and Near Eastern cultures.
Horse breeding was important in Colchian society, and the kingdom supplied cavalry to neighboring kingdoms. Colchian horses were prized for their endurance in mountainous terrain.

The coastline of ancient Colchis was dotted with small harbors and fishing villages. Beyond the shore lay impenetrable swamps and forests that discouraged invasion but supported rich hunting and foraging.

By 300 BC, the Kingdom of Colchis was beginning to fragment, giving way to new powers. But its legacy — its gold, its myths, and its fierce independence — would echo through Georgian history for three thousand years.
Around 300 BC, a new kingdom arose in eastern Georgia: Iberia, known to its people as Kartli. This kingdom would become the core of Georgian national identity for all the centuries that followed.

King Pharnavaz I, the legendary founder of the Iberian Kingdom, unified the eastern Georgian tribes and established a centralized state. He is said to have created the Georgian script, giving his people a written language of their own.

Mtskheta, at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Aragvi rivers, became the ancient capital of Iberia. Its strategic position made it a crossroads of civilizations — Roman, Persian, and Greek influences all flowed through its streets.

The Kingdom of Iberia sat at the crossroads of empires — Rome to the west, Persia to the east, and Armenia to the south. Georgian kings became master diplomats, playing great powers against each other to preserve independence.

Uplistsikhe, the 'Fortress of the Lord,' is an ancient cave city carved into a rocky cliff along the Mtkvari River. Its tunnels, halls, and churches span centuries of Georgian history, from pagan temples to early Christian worship.

Roman influence reached Iberia in the 1st century AD, when Pompey's legions marched through the Caucasus. The Iberian kings became Roman allies — but also maintained ties with Persia, a dangerous balancing act.

In 327 AD, St. Nino of Cappadocia brought Christianity to the Kingdom of Iberia. According to tradition, King Mirian III was converted after witnessing a miracle, making Georgia one of the first Christian nations on Earth.

St. Nino is said to have healed the Queen of Iberia from a grave illness, which led to the royal family's conversion. A simple cross made of grapevine branches, bound with her own hair, became Georgia's most sacred relic.

The Svetitskhoveli Cathedral in Mtskheta, built over the site where the robe of Christ was allegedly buried, became the spiritual heart of Georgian Christianity. It remains one of the most important churches in the Caucasus to this day.

The Armenian-Georgian relationship in antiquity was complex — the two kingdoms shared a faith but often competed for territory and influence. Dynastic marriages between Armenian and Georgian royal families were common political tools.

Persian cultural influence shaped Iberian court life, art, and architecture for centuries. Many Georgian nobles spoke Persian and adopted Persian customs, even as the kingdom fiercely resisted political domination from the Sassanid Empire.

King Vakhtang Gorgasali, who ruled in the late 5th and early 6th centuries, is one of Georgia's greatest national heroes. He fought tirelessly against Persian domination and earned his name 'Gorgasali' — meaning 'Wolf's Head' — for his fierce temperament.

According to legend, King Vakhtang founded Tbilisi when his falcon caught a pheasant that fell into a hot spring. The king was so impressed by the warm sulfur waters that he ordered a city built there. The name Tbilisi comes from the Georgian word for 'warm.'

Tbilisi quickly grew into a major trading city, benefiting from its position on the Silk Road. Caravans from China, India, Persia, and the Byzantine Empire all passed through its markets, bringing goods and ideas from across the known world.

The Roman-Parthian rivalry created both danger and opportunity for Iberia. Georgian kings shifted alliances between Rome and Persia as circumstances demanded, a precarious strategy that sometimes paid off and sometimes brought devastating retaliation.

Early Georgian Christian art blended Byzantine, Persian, and local styles into something entirely unique. Stone crosses, illuminated manuscripts, and church frescoes from this period reveal a sophisticated artistic tradition.

The Georgian alphabet, one of only 14 unique alphabets in the world, was developed during this era. Its three scripts — Asomtavruli, Nuskhuri, and Mkhedruli — reflect centuries of literary and cultural development.

Daily life in Iberian towns centered around the church, the market, and the fortress. Most people were farmers, growing wheat, grapes, and beans, while artisans produced pottery, metalwork, and textiles of remarkable quality.

The transition from antiquity to the Middle Ages was turbulent for Iberia. Arab armies appeared on the horizon, and the old kingdom would soon face its greatest challenge. Yet the faith, language, and identity forged in this era would prove unbreakable.

By 630 AD, the ancient Kingdom of Iberia had endured for nearly a millennium. It had survived Roman legions, Persian armies, and internal strife. Now, a new storm was gathering — but the Georgian spirit was ready to face it.
The 7th century brought catastrophe to Georgia. Arab armies, surging out of the Arabian Peninsula, shattered the old order and brought the Caucasus under Islamic rule for the first time.
In 645 AD, Arab forces captured Tbilisi after a fierce siege. The city became the capital of the Tbilisi Emirate, a Muslim-ruled state that would dominate eastern Georgia for over four centuries.

The Tbilisi Emirate was a cosmopolitan city where Muslim, Christian, and Jewish communities lived side by side. Georgian nobles who converted to Islam could retain their positions, creating a complex web of loyalty and resistance.

In the western regions of Georgia, the Kingdom of Abkhazia emerged as a powerful state. Free from direct Arab control, it preserved Georgian Christian culture and expanded its influence along the Black Sea coast.

The Bagrationi dynasty, which would rule Georgia for a thousand years, began its rise during this chaotic period. Originally minor princes in the southern regions, they gradually consolidated power through war, marriage, and sheer persistence.

Mountain fortresses became the last refuges of Georgian independence. Inaccessible castles perched on cliff tops allowed Georgian princes to resist Arab authority and preserve their culture in isolated highland valleys.

The Byzantine Empire, Georgia's fellow Christian power, was both ally and rival. Byzantine emperors sought to extend their influence into the Caucasus, sometimes supporting Georgian rulers and sometimes undermining them.

Kakheti, a region in eastern Georgia, maintained a fragile independence through diplomacy and guerrilla warfare. Its rulers built a network of small fortresses that made outright conquest prohibitively costly for invaders.

The Battle of Shirimni in 1021 was a devastating defeat for King Bagrat III, who lost much of his army fighting the Byzantine Emperor Basil II. Georgia's dream of unification seemed to slip further away.

The Battle of Sasireti in 1042 pitted Georgian princes against each other in a bloody civil war. King Bagrat IV narrowly survived, but the conflict weakened Georgia at a time when new threats were approaching from the east.

Seljuk Turks, migrating from Central Asia, began raiding Georgian territory in the mid-11th century. Their swift horsemen were devastating in open battle, and Georgian armies struggled to adapt to this new style of warfare.

Despite the political turmoil, Georgian monks preserved and enriched the nation's literary heritage. Monasteries in the Georgian wilderness became centers of learning, producing beautiful manuscripts and original theological works.

The cave monasteries of Davit Gareja, carved into a barren desert landscape, became a symbol of spiritual resistance. Monks lived in tiny cells carved into rock, praying and copying manuscripts by candlelight.

Georgian builders developed a distinctive architectural style during this period, characterized by domed churches with intricate stone carvings. These churches, built in remote mountain areas, were designed to be both places of worship and fortresses.

The population suffered greatly during the centuries of invasion. Villages were destroyed, crops burned, and thousands were carried off into slavery. Yet the Georgian people refused to abandon their highland homeland.

Diplomacy became a survival tool. Georgian rulers sent envoys to Constantinople, Baghdad, and even distant courts in Europe, seeking allies and buying time. One notable embassy reached the Holy Roman Emperor in the 10th century.

The Tao-Klarjeti region, in what is now northeastern Turkey, became the cradle of the Georgian cultural renaissance. Under the Bagrationi princes, churches, monasteries, and academies flourished in this frontier zone.

Georgian liturgical music evolved during this period into one of the world's most distinctive choral traditions. Three-part harmony, unique to Georgia, developed in the monasteries and churches of this era.

By the late 11th century, the Seljuk threat had become existential. Large parts of southern Georgia were occupied, and the kingdom seemed on the verge of collapse. A savior was needed — and one would soon emerge.

In 1089, a young king named David ascended the throne of a fractured, war-torn Georgia. He would become known as David the Builder, and under his leadership, Georgia would enter its greatest age. The long night of invasions was ending.

King David IV, only 16 when he took the throne, inherited a kingdom on its knees. Seljuk Turks occupied the best lands, the treasury was empty, and many nobles refused to obey the crown. His reign would transform Georgia forever.

David the Builder reformed the Georgian army, creating the monaspa — an elite royal guard loyal directly to the king rather than to feudal lords. These disciplined troops, trained in new tactics, became the backbone of Georgia's military revival.

David expelled the Seljuk Turks from key cities through a series of brilliant campaigns. He captured Tbilisi in 1122, ending nearly five centuries of Arab rule and making the city Georgia's capital once more.

The Battle of Didgori, fought on August 12, 1121, is Georgia's greatest military victory. David's army of 56,000 defeated a Seljuk force of 300,000 (by contemporary accounts) on a dusty plain west of Tbilisi. The battle opened the road to Tbilisi and Georgian supremacy.

David the Builder founded the Gelati Monastery and Academy in 1106, which became the greatest center of learning in the Caucasus. Scholars from across the Orthodox world gathered there to study theology, philosophy, astronomy, and medicine.

Under David's rule, Georgia's economy flourished. Trade routes were secured, new towns were founded, and agriculture recovered. Gold and silver coins bearing the king's image circulated widely across the region.

David resettled Turkic nomadic tribes (known as Kipchaks) into Georgia, giving them land in exchange for military service. These 40,000 families provided crucial cavalry for the Georgian army and became loyal subjects.
Queen Tamar, who ruled from 1184 to 1213, is Georgia's most celebrated monarch. She was crowned in a unique ceremony — the first woman to rule Georgia in her own right, not as a regent or consort.

Under Queen Tamar, Georgia reached the peak of its power and cultural achievement. The kingdom stretched from the Black Sea to the Caspian, from the Caucasus Mountains to northern Iran. Tamar was called 'King of Kings and Queen of Queens.'

Shota Rustaveli, Georgia's greatest poet, served at Queen Tamar's court. His epic poem 'The Knight in the Panther's Skin' is a masterpiece of world literature, celebrating love, friendship, and courage in a style unmatched in medieval writing.

Vardzia, the magnificent cave city in southern Georgia, was expanded by Queen Tamar into a vast underground complex with hundreds of rooms, churches, and a sophisticated irrigation system. It could house up to 20,000 people.

Zakaria Mkhargrdzeli, Tamar's most trusted general, led Georgian armies to victories across the Caucasus and into Anatolia. He founded the powerful Mkhargrdzeli dynasty and helped establish Georgian dominance over much of the southern Caucasus.

Georgian ecclesiastical architecture reached its zenith in the Golden Age. The domed cathedrals of this period — with their elegant proportions and exquisite stone carvings — are considered among the finest medieval buildings in the world.

The Georgian Orthodox Church became autocephalous (self-governing) during this period, a source of immense national pride. Church leaders and the royal court worked closely together, though tensions over the balance of power sometimes flared.

Georgian monks established monasteries across the eastern Mediterranean — on Mount Sinai, in Jerusalem, on the island of Cyprus, and in Bulgaria. These outposts spread Georgian culture and maintained connections with the wider Christian world.

Trade flourished under the Golden Age. Georgian merchants traveled to India, Egypt, and Byzantium, while foreign traders filled the markets of Tbilisi. The city became one of the great cosmopolitan centers of the medieval world.

Georgian enamel work, known as cloisonné, reached extraordinary heights during this period. Icons and crosses decorated with brilliant colored enamel were created for churches across the kingdom, and some survive in museums today.

The concept of Georgian unity — 'ertiani, shvidi, didi Sakartvelo' (one, strong, great Georgia) — became a powerful political ideal during the Golden Age. It inspired loyalty across the diverse regions of the kingdom.

Queen Rusudan, Tamar's successor, faced the beginning of Georgia's decline. The Mongol invasions, which would devastate the kingdom, were already probing Georgian defenses. The Golden Age was drawing to a close.

The Georgian Golden Age lasted barely a century and a half, but it produced achievements that still define Georgian national identity. The language, the faith, the architecture, and the literature of this extraordinary era remain Georgia's greatest treasures.

In the 1220s, Mongol scouts first appeared on Georgia's borders. These riders from the steppes of Central Asia had already conquered the greatest empires of Asia — now they turned their gaze toward the Caucasus.

The first major Mongol invasion of Georgia struck in 1222, catching the Georgian army off guard. At the Battle of Khunan on the Kotman River, the Mongols routed King George IV Lasha's forces, killing 10,000 Georgian soldiers.

Georgia's response was hampered by internal divisions. The court was split between those who wanted to fight and those who advocated submission. Queen Rusudan, who came to power in 1223, struggled to unite the nobility against the common threat.
In 1236, the full Mongol invasion arrived. Georgian resistance collapsed as one city after another fell. Tbilisi was captured, and the Mongols imposed devastating terms on the defeated kingdom.

By 1243, Georgia had been completely subjugated. King David VI was forced to accept Mongol suzerainty, and Georgia became a vassal state required to pay heavy tribute in gold, silver, horses, and soldiers.

Georgian soldiers were conscripted into Mongol armies and sent to fight in campaigns across Asia and Europe. Many died in distant battles — in China, in Iraq, in Eastern Europe — serving an empire that had crushed their homeland.

The Mongol tribute system drained Georgia of its wealth. Taxes were crushing, and failure to pay brought brutal reprisals. Entire villages were destroyed to punish communities that fell behind on payments.

Georgia was divided by the Mongols into rival administrative zones, each governed by different Mongol princes. This deliberate fragmentation undermined Georgian unity and encouraged internal rivalries that would plague the kingdom for generations.

The Ilkhanate, the Mongol state that ruled Georgia, gradually adopted Persian culture and Islam. Georgian nobles found themselves navigating an increasingly Islamic court culture while trying to preserve their own Christian identity.

Population decimation was catastrophic. Contemporary estimates suggest Georgia lost up to two-thirds of its population during the Mongol period — from war, famine, disease, and mass enslavement. Towns shrank to shadows of their former selves.

Mountain regions became refuges for those fleeing Mongol rule. Svaneti, Tusheti, and Pshavi — remote highland areas — preserved Georgian culture and independence while the lowlands suffered under occupation.

The Svan towers of Upper Svaneti, tall stone defensive structures built to protect villages from both invaders and avalanches, became symbols of resistance. They still stand today, some over a thousand years old, guarding their mountain communities.

Georgian monasteries, hidden in remote valleys and mountain gorges, became centers of cultural preservation. Monks continued to copy manuscripts and teach the young, ensuring that Georgian learning would survive the occupation.
Despite everything, Georgian art did not die. Illuminated manuscripts from the Mongol period show a quiet, refined beauty — the work of artists who created beauty even in the darkest of times.

The Golden Horde, the western Mongol khanate, periodically contested control of Georgia with the Ilkhanate. Georgian lands became a battleground between rival Mongol factions, adding another layer of suffering for the civilian population.

Occasional rebellions flared against Mongol rule, most notably under King Demetrius II in the late 13th century. These uprisings were brutally suppressed, and Demetrius himself was executed by the Mongols in 1289.

The Black Death, which swept through the Mongol Empire in the 1340s, devastated Georgia's already weakened population. It was one of many catastrophes that made the Mongol period one of the most traumatic in Georgian history.

As Mongol power waned in the late 14th century, new threats emerged. Timur (Tamerlane) invaded Georgia eight times between 1386 and 1403, devastating the country with campaigns of exceptional brutality even by Mongol standards.

By the late 15th century, Georgia had fragmented into three separate kingdoms — Kartli, Kakheti, and Imereti — plus several principalities. The great unified kingdom of the Golden Age was gone, perhaps forever.

Yet Georgia survived. Language, faith, and cultural memory held the nation together through two and a half centuries of catastrophe. The resilience forged in this terrible period would sustain Georgians through every trial that followed.

In the 16th century, Georgia found itself caught between two great Islamic empires: the Ottoman Turks to the west and Safavid Persia to the east. Both claimed Georgian territory, and both fought to control it.

The three kingdoms of Kartli, Kakheti, and Imereti each developed their own distinct character. Kartli and Kakheti in the east looked toward Persia, while Imereti in the west fell increasingly under Ottoman influence.

Georgian kings were forced to accept Islam, at least nominally, to appease their Persian overlords. These conversions were often strategic — many rulers continued to practice Christianity in private while maintaining a Muslim court persona.

Georgian slaves were highly valued in both Ottoman and Persian markets. Slave raids depopulated border regions, and many Georgians rose to high positions in foreign courts — some even becoming powerful viziers and military commanders.

Despite the constant warfare, Georgian culture experienced a remarkable revival in the 17th and 18th centuries. Poetry flourished, new churches were built, and scholars produced important works of history and theology.

King Teimuraz I of Kakheti, a poet-king who ruled in the early 17th century, wrote some of Georgia's most beautiful and heartbreaking verse. His poems, many written in captivity, mourn the suffering of his homeland with haunting intensity.

The Georgian Orthodox Church, though persecuted, maintained its role as the guardian of national identity. Even when churches were destroyed and clergy killed, the faith endured in the hearts of the common people.

Diplomatic relations with Russia, Georgia's fellow Orthodox power to the north, began in earnest in the 16th century. Georgian kings saw Russia as a potential protector against Muslim empires, though help was often slow in coming.

King Erekle II, who ruled Kartli-Kakheti from 1762 to 1798, was the last great Georgian monarch. Intelligent, brave, and determined, he dreamed of reunifying Georgia and freeing it from foreign domination.

Erekle II forged a military alliance with Catherine the Great of Russia in 1783, the Treaty of Georgievsk. It promised Russian protection in exchange for Georgian cooperation in foreign policy. It would prove to be a fateful decision.

The Treaty of Georgievsk was celebrated by many Georgians as a guarantee of security. But Russia's promises would prove hollow when Georgia most needed help, and the treaty became a stepping stone to annexation rather than protection.
Agha Mohammad Khan, the founder of the Qajar dynasty of Persia, demanded that Georgia submit to his rule. When King Erekle refused, the Shah gathered a massive army and marched on Tbilisi.

The Battle of Krtsanisi, fought on September 11, 1795, was one of Georgia's darkest hours. Agha Mohammad Khan's Persian army crushed the Georgian forces and sacked Tbilisi, burning the city to the ground and slaughtering thousands of its inhabitants.

King Erekle survived the battle but was heartbroken. His dying words reportedly expressed regret at trusting Russia, which had failed to send the promised military assistance during Georgia's hour of greatest need.

In the aftermath of Krtsanisi, Georgia was devastated. Tbilisi lay in ruins, the economy was shattered, and the population was traumatized. Many believed that without Russian protection, Georgia could not survive.

Daily life in 18th-century Georgia revolved around the village, the church, and the family. Despite the political turmoil, people farmed their fields, celebrated their festivals, and maintained their traditions with stubborn devotion.

Georgian polyphonic singing, a tradition that may date back thousands of years, was kept alive in villages across the country. These haunting three-part harmonies, sung at feasts and funerals, expressed the soul of the Georgian people.

The Georgian supra, or feast, was (and remains) a cornerstone of social life. Led by a toastmaster (tamada), these elaborate meals feature toasts to God, country, family, and friends — a ritual of community and belonging.

In 1800, King Giorgi XII, Erekle's son and successor, formally requested that Georgia be annexed by Russia. It was a desperate act by a dying kingdom, seeking survival through absorption into a larger empire.

In January 1801, Tsar Paul I of Russia issued a manifesto abolishing the Kingdom of Kartli-Kakheti and incorporating Georgia into the Russian Empire. Georgian royal rule, which had endured for over two thousand years, came to an end.

The Russian annexation of 1801 was a turning point in Georgian history. Some welcomed it as protection from Persia and the Ottomans, while others saw it as the loss of independence they had fought so long to preserve.

The Russian imperial administration abolished the Georgian monarchy, the Bagrationi dynasty, and the traditional nobility's privileges. Georgian princes were gradually incorporated into the Russian aristocracy, their power diluted and their status diminished.

Tbilisi was transformed under Russian rule. Wide boulevards, European-style buildings, and new administrative centers changed the face of the old city. Yet in its narrow streets and hidden courtyards, traditional Georgian life continued.

The Transcaucasian Railway, completed in the 1880s, connected Tbilisi to the Black Sea port of Batumi and to Russia's rail network. It brought economic development but also tightened Russia's grip on the region.

Ilia Chavchavadze, born in 1837, is considered the father of the modern Georgian national movement. A writer, journalist, and politician, he fought tirelessly for Georgian language rights, cultural revival, and social reform.

Ilia Chavchavadze founded the newspaper 'Iveria' in 1877, which became the voice of Georgian national awakening. Through its pages, he advocated for education in the Georgian language and the preservation of Georgian culture against Russification.

Akaki Tsereteli, Georgia's greatest folk poet, captured the spirit of the common people in his verse. His poems and songs, written in vivid Georgian, celebrated the beauty of the homeland and the dignity of the peasant.

Serfdom was abolished in Georgia in 1864, three years after its abolition in Russia. While it freed peasants from legal bondage, the land reforms that accompanied it were inadequate, and many rural Georgians remained desperately poor.

Batumi, on the Black Sea coast, became Georgia's most important port and a boomtown of the late 19th century. Oil from Baku flowed through its pipelines to the waiting ships, and the city grew rich and cosmopolitan.

The national awakening of the 1860s-1890s saw a flourishing of Georgian culture. New schools taught in Georgian, theaters staged Georgian plays, and scholars rediscovered and published ancient Georgian texts that had been forgotten for centuries.

The Svaneti region, with its medieval towers and remote mountain villages, preserved traditions that had disappeared elsewhere. Russian ethnographers and European travelers were fascinated by its archaic culture and dramatic landscapes.

Georgian wine-making traditions, among the oldest in the world (dating back over 8,000 years), were maintained throughout the Russian period. The unique qvevri method — fermenting wine in large clay vessels buried underground — was kept alive in village cellars.

In 1907, the charismatic Ilia Chavchavadze was assassinated by unknown gunmen. His death shocked the nation and is still mourned as a national tragedy. He remains Georgia's most revered historical figure, honored on the 100-lari banknote.

The early 20th century brought rapid industrialization to Georgia. Factories, railways, and oil refineries transformed the economy, but the benefits flowed largely to Russian and foreign investors rather than to the Georgian population.

Georgian social democratic parties, influenced by Marxist ideas, gained popularity among workers and intellectuals. The Menshevik faction became dominant in Georgia, advocating for a more gradual and democratic path to socialism.

World War I brought the Caucasus Front to Georgia's borders. Georgian soldiers fought in the Russian army against the Ottoman Empire, and the war created economic hardship and political upheaval throughout the region.
The 1917 Russian Revolution shattered the old order. As the Tsarist empire collapsed, Georgian leaders saw an opportunity to reclaim their independence after more than a century of Russian rule.

In the chaotic aftermath of the revolution, Georgia briefly became part of the Transcaucasian Democratic Federative Republic, sharing power with Armenia and Azerbaijan. But this uneasy alliance would not last long.

As the Russian Empire disintegrated, Georgian politicians and intellectuals debated the future. Should Georgia remain part of a larger federation, or declare full independence? The moment of decision was approaching.
On May 26, 1918, Georgia declared its independence. After 117 years of Russian rule, Georgians would govern themselves once again. But the new republic would face immediate challenges from all sides.

On May 26, 1918, the National Council of Georgia declared independence from the collapsing Russian Empire. Noe Jordania, a social democrat, became the head of the new government, leading the first independent Georgian republic in over a century.

Georgia's new constitution was remarkably progressive for its time. It granted universal suffrage — including to women — and established a parliamentary democracy. Georgia was one of the first countries in Europe to give women the vote.

Noe Jordania, the leader of the Georgian Mensheviks, was a pragmatic politician who tried to balance socialist reforms with the practical demands of governing a fragile new state. He had led Georgia's social democratic movement since the early 1900s.

The new republic faced enormous challenges: economic collapse, border disputes, ethnic tensions, and hostile neighbors on all sides. Armenia, Azerbaijan, and the White Russian forces all posed potential threats.

Georgia's multi-ethnic democracy attempted to protect the rights of all its citizens. Abkhazians, Ossetians, Armenians, Azerbaijanis, and Russians all lived within Georgia's borders, and the republic tried — imperfectly — to accommodate their aspirations.

The Georgian army, hastily assembled, fought border wars against Armenia, Azerbaijan, and White Russian forces. Despite limited resources, Georgian troops managed to secure most of the country's claimed territory.

Land reform was one of the republic's most important achievements. Large estates were broken up and distributed to peasant farmers, fulfilling a long-standing demand that had driven much of Georgia's rural unrest under Russian rule.

Tbilisi in 1918-1921 was a vibrant, cosmopolitan city. Cafes, theaters, and newspapers flourished despite the difficult conditions. The city attracted intellectuals and artists from across the Caucasus.

In January 1920, Georgia achieved a major diplomatic victory when the Supreme Council of the Allied Powers de facto recognized its independence. It was a sign that the international community took Georgia seriously as a sovereign state.

In 1921, Georgia was admitted to the League of Nations, the international body created after World War I to maintain peace. Georgia was one of only a handful of small nations to gain recognition from this prestigious organization.
The republic's government invested in education, opening new schools across the country. Literacy rates began to rise after centuries of neglect, and Georgian-language publishing flourished.

Economic conditions remained difficult throughout the republic's brief existence. War, blockade, and the disruption of trade routes caused shortages of food and basic goods, leading to popular discontent.

The Georgian-Ossetian conflict of 1918-1920 was one of the republic's most painful challenges. Ethnic tensions in the Tskhinvali region erupted into violence, and the government's response was criticized by both sides.

Karlo Chkheidze, a prominent Georgian social democrat, represented Georgia at international socialist congresses, arguing for the right of small nations to self-determination. His eloquent advocacy won sympathy in European capitals.

Despite its difficulties, the Georgian republic achieved significant reforms in workers' rights, women's equality, and local self-government. These achievements would be largely erased after the Soviet annexation.

Georgian intellectuals in the republic debated the country's identity and future. Was Georgia European or Asian? Should it look west or east? These questions, unresolved in 1921, continue to shape Georgian politics today.

The Bolshevik government in Moscow never accepted Georgia's independence. Lenin and his allies viewed the Menshevik government as a bourgeois deviation and plotted to bring Georgia back under communist control.
In February 1921, the Red Army invaded Georgia. The Georgian army, outnumbered and outgunned, fought bravely but could not stop the Soviet advance. Tbilisi fell after fierce street fighting.

As Soviet forces closed in, the Georgian government fled into exile. Noe Jordania and other leaders eventually settled in France, where they would spend decades advocating for Georgia's freedom from abroad.

The First Democratic Republic of Georgia lasted less than three years, from May 1918 to February 1921. But its memory — as a brief, shining experiment in democracy — would inspire generations of Georgians who dreamed of freedom.
Georgia was forcibly incorporated into the Soviet Union in 1921. The Georgian SSR became one of the fifteen republics of the USSR, nominally self-governing but in reality controlled from Moscow.

Ioseb Jughashvili — better known to the world as Joseph Stalin — was born in the Georgian town of Gori in 1878. The son of a cobbler, he rose from obscure revolutionary to become the absolute ruler of the Soviet Union.

Stalin's connection to Georgia was complex. He spoke Georgian throughout his life, loved Georgian food and wine, and maintained close ties with Georgian associates. Yet his policies would bring untold suffering to his homeland.

The Great Purge of 1937-1938 hit Georgia disproportionately hard. Many of Stalin's Georgian rivals and associates were executed, including leading Bolsheviks who had helped build the Soviet state. Fear permeated every level of society.

During World War II, approximately 350,000 Georgian soldiers died fighting in the Red Army. Georgians fought in every major battle on the Eastern Front, from Stalingrad to Berlin, paying a terrible price for the Soviet victory.

Soviet modernization brought factories, roads, and schools to Georgia. The republic industrialized rapidly, and cities like Tbilisi, Kutaisi, and Rustavi became major industrial centers. But progress came at the cost of environmental damage and cultural suppression.

Tbilisi was transformed under Soviet rule into a modern metropolis. New avenues, public buildings, and a metro system changed the city's character, though the old town with its carved wooden balconies was partially preserved.

Georgian cinema experienced a golden age in the Soviet period. Directors like Tengiz Abuladze, Otar Ioseliani, and Georgi Danelia created films that were celebrated internationally for their artistry and subtle resistance to Soviet conformity.
Soviet Georgia was known as a relatively prosperous and relaxed republic, where wine flowed freely and the black market thrived. Russians joked that 'Georgia is not a republic — it's a way of life.'

The Georgian railway network, expanded under Soviet rule, connected the republic to the rest of the USSR. Georgian trains, famous for their restaurant cars serving local wine and cuisine, were among the best in the Soviet Union.

In 1956, the death of Stalin triggered mass protests in Tbilisi. Thousands of Georgians — many of whom still revered Stalin as a national hero — marched through the streets to commemorate him. Soviet troops opened fire, killing dozens.

Georgian dissidents, though fewer in number than in the Baltic republics, kept the spirit of resistance alive. Writers, artists, and religious believers quietly challenged Soviet authority through their work and their faith.

The Georgian Orthodox Church, severely persecuted under Stalin and Khrushchev, experienced a modest revival from the 1960s onward. Churches that had been closed were gradually reopened, and religious life began to recover.

In 1978, thousands of Georgians took to the streets to protest a proposed change to the Georgian constitution that would have downgraded the status of the Georgian language. The Soviet government, surprised by the strength of the protest, backed down.

The 1978 language protests were a watershed moment. They showed that popular mobilization could force Moscow to retreat, and they emboldened Georgian nationalists to push for greater autonomy and eventual independence.

Zviad Gamsakhurdia, a literary scholar and dissident, became the leading figure of Georgia's national revival movement in the 1980s. His passionate advocacy for Georgian independence attracted a growing following as the Soviet Union weakened.

The national revival movement of the late 1980s brought thousands of Georgians into the streets. Demonstrations, hunger strikes, and mass rallies demanded greater autonomy, environmental protection, and ultimately full independence.

On April 9, 1989, Soviet troops attacked a peaceful pro-independence demonstration in Tbilisi, killing 21 people, mostly young women. The April 9 Tragedy became a symbol of Soviet brutality and a rallying cry for the independence movement.
The April 9 massacre horrified the Soviet public and the international community. Even some Communist Party officials in Georgia broke with Moscow. The Soviet grip on Georgia was visibly weakening.

On April 9, 1991 — exactly two years after the tragedy — Georgia declared its independence from the Soviet Union. The Soviet century was over. After 70 years, Georgians were once again masters of their own fate.

Independence in 1991 brought joy but also immediate crisis. The Soviet economy collapsed, leaving Georgia without reliable electricity, food supplies, or functioning institutions. The euphoria of freedom was quickly tempered by the harsh realities of state-building.

Zviad Gamsakhurdia, elected as Georgia's first president in 1991, proved to be a charismatic but divisive leader. His authoritarian tendencies and nationalist rhetoric alienated many supporters and provoked opposition from powerful factions.

Civil war erupted in late 1991, pitting Gamsakhurdia's supporters against a coalition of opposition forces. Tbilisi became a battlefield, and the fighting destroyed parts of the city center. Gamsakhurdia was driven from power and eventually fled the country.

The Abkhazia war of 1992-1993 was one of the most traumatic conflicts in modern Georgian history. Georgian forces fought to retain control of the breakaway region, but after bitter fighting, Abkhaz separatists — with Russian support — drove Georgian forces out.

The Abkhazia war displaced hundreds of thousands of ethnic Georgians from their homes. Many fled to other parts of Georgia, where they became refugees in their own country. The wounds of this conflict have never fully healed.

The economy collapsed in the early 1990s. Hyperinflation wiped out savings, factories closed, and basic services became unreliable. Many Georgians survived through subsistence farming and the informal economy.

Eduard Shevardnadze, the former Soviet foreign minister, became Georgia's leader in 1995. His experience on the world stage brought international credibility, but corruption and stagnation increasingly undermined his government.

Despite the difficulties, Georgian culture experienced a revival in the 1990s. New literary journals, theater productions, and music festivals reflected both the pain and the resilience of a society in transition.

The Rose Revolution of November 2003 was a pivotal moment in Georgia's modern history. Mass protests, led by a young lawyer named Mikheil Saakashvili, forced Eduard Shevardnadze to resign and opened the door to sweeping reforms.

Mikheil Saakashvili, elected president in 2004, launched an ambitious program of reforms. Police reform eliminated the notoriously corrupt traffic police, and anti-corruption measures transformed the business environment.

Saakashvili's government rebuilt Georgia's infrastructure and pursued an aggressive policy of Euro-Atlantic integration. New roads, modern buildings, and improved public services changed the face of Tbilisi and other cities.

The August 2008 war with Russia was a devastating shock. After Georgian forces moved into the breakaway territory of South Ossetia, Russia responded with a full-scale military invasion that Georgian forces could not resist.

The 2008 war resulted in the loss of South Ossetia and Abkhazia from Georgian control and the displacement of additional civilians. Russia recognized both territories as independent states, though few other countries followed suit.

In the aftermath of the 2008 war, Georgia redoubled its efforts to join NATO and the European Union. The conflict underscored the vulnerability of small states facing large neighbors and strengthened the argument for deeper Western integration.

The 2012 parliamentary elections brought the Georgian Dream party to power, ending Saakashvili's decade in government. The peaceful transfer of power was hailed as a sign of Georgia's democratic maturation.

Georgia's tourism industry has boomed in recent years, with visitors drawn to the country's ancient churches, dramatic mountain scenery, vibrant cities, and world-renowned wine and cuisine. Tourism has become a pillar of the economy.

Georgian wine, made using the ancient qvevri method, has been recognized by UNESCO as part of the world's intangible cultural heritage. Georgian wines are now exported worldwide, winning international awards and attracting wine tourists.

Euro-Atlantic integration remains Georgia's strategic priority. In 2022, Georgia applied for EU membership, a dream that would have seemed impossible a decade earlier. The European Commission recommended granting Georgia candidate status.

Georgia's democratic consolidation faces ongoing challenges. Political polarization, judicial reform, and the balance between economic development and social justice are all subjects of intense national debate.

From the ancient kingdom of Colchis to the modern Republic of Georgia, the Georgian people have endured invasions, empires, and catastrophes — and have emerged each time with their language, faith, and identity intact. The story of Georgia is, above all, a story of survival.
